title: Does mindfulness-based stress reduction training have an impact on the occupational burnout and stress experienced by nurses? A randomized controlled trial
journal: International archives of occupational and environmental health
headline: Does mindfulness-based stress reduction training have an impact on the occupational burnout and stress experie
summary: A randomized controlled trial of Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) examined emotional wellbeing. In an effort to combat burnout, a study investigates Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) training for nurses.
keyFindings: In an effort to combat burnout, a study investigates Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) training for nurses.
The goal is to reduce stress and improve job satisfaction, ultimately enhancing patient care.
limitations: The randomization process, utilizing sealed envelopes, ensured unbiased allocation to intervention or control groups.
Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.

The practice studied (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared directed-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Does mindfulness-based stress reduction training have an impact on the occupational burnout and stress experie
journalName: International archives of occupational and environmental health
aiSnippet: RCT, International archives of occupational and environmental health (2025). In an effort to combat burnout, a study investigates Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) training for nurses. The goal is to reduce stress and improve job satisfaction, ultimately enhancing patient care.
whyItMatters: MoM shares directed attention (the focused-attention component) with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
